name: Boulevardier

spec.method_detail: Stir with ice until well-chilled. Strain into chilled coupe or over large rock in rocks glass.

history: Erskine Gwynne, an American expatriate in Paris, published the Boulevardier in his Paris literary magazine 'The Boulevardier' in 1927. It's essentially a Negroni with bourbon replacing gin — a natural riff given the era's preference for whiskey. The name refers to a fashionable man-about-town. Harry McElhone included it in 'Barflies and Cocktails' the same year.
